petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented individual seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts or sciences as an executive director of a future technology ethics department. His background is in Christian ethics and theology, having served as editor of Crucible (a publication of the Church of England's Board for Social Responsibility), director of the Center for the Study of Theology at the University of Essex, and a member of various ethics-related organizations in Great Britain. The petition has a complex procedural history, including a prior AAO remand, and the petitioner failed to respond to a subsequent Notice of Intent to Deny (NOID), apparently having moved without notifying CIS as required by statute.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner's CV listed membership in several organizations, but the record only documented three: (1) a prospectus for an unnamed Foundation and a copy of its publication 'Foundations' listing him as a member of an advisory panel, plus a February 14, 1997 letter from the Foundation requesting he join its 'Council'; (2) a letter regarding the Society of Black Lawyers' launch of the Black Legal Agenda indicating interest in inviting 'senior members' of the society's board, especially the petitioner, to the launch; and (3) evidence of membership in the Society for the Study of Christian Ethics, which the petitioner asserted is the main professional body in Great Britain for Christian ethicists and claimed that membership requires nomination, seconding, and formal approval by existing members.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record contained no documentation of the membership criteria for the Foundation or the Society of Black Lawyers, nor other evidence that outstanding achievements are a prerequisite to membership. For the Society for the Study of Christian Ethics, the record contained no evidence to corroborate the petitioner's claims about the nomination and approval process. The director's NOID had requested submission of the bylaws, membership criteria, and other evidence establishing that the organizations require outstanding achievements as judged by recognized national or international experts, but no such evidence was received. The AAO agreed with the director that the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a certificate confirming his inclusion in Who's Who in the World, 17th Edition 2000.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence that inclusion in Who's Who in the World reflects the sustained national or international acclaim requisite to classification as an alien with extraordinary ability. The NOID requested additional evidence of the petitioner's eligibility under this criterion, but no supplemental evidence was received. The director correctly found that the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted two letters relevant to this criterion. A letter from a professor at the University of Essex indicated that the petitioner was the editor of Crucible, a publication of the Church of England's Board for Social Responsibility, and claimed the publication is highly regarded. The letter stated that as editor, the petitioner coordinated and formulated Church policy on sensitive issues. A second letter from the secretary of the board confirmed the petitioner served as editor for Crucible and listed his name as the editor to whom articles should be submitted, and praised the petitioner's editorial pieces.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Although the second letter praised the petitioner's editorial pieces, it did not state how the editor of Crucible is chosen. The record also contained no evidence of Crucible's circulation. The NOID requested additional evidence to verify the claims in both letters, but no supplemental evidence was received. Additionally, the prior AAO decision had noted that signatures on photocopies of six letters appeared to be digitally reproduced and required the director to request original letters with original signatures pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(5), but none were received. The letters were insufficient to establish that the petitioner's work as an editor reflects the requisite sustained acclaim. The director's decision discussed the evidence but did not state whether the petitioner met this criterion. The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The director's present decision did not discuss the evidence submitted under this criterion. The NOID had requested pre-existing independent evidence of the petitioner's contributions to his field as well as evidence that the petitioner had actually formulated the opinions advanced in position papers that he purportedly helped write. No additional evidence was received in response.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The previous AAO decision had concurred with the director's original determination that the petitioner did not meet this criterion. Although the director's present decision did not discuss eligibility under this criterion, the AAO found this oversight did not prejudice the petitioner because he met no other regulatory criteria. Because no additional evidence was received in response to the NOID, the record did not establish that the petitioner meets this criterion for the reasons discussed in the previous AAO decision.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence relating to a publication titled 'The Situated Soul: Wisdom and [truncated]' through a publishing company's commissioning editor. He also submitted a database printout regarding his thesis, copies of Crucible and Essex Papers in Theology and Society containing his commentary, copies of two other opinion pieces and a book review written by the petitioner, and copies of 'Submission of the Church of England Board for Social Responsibility to the Royal Commission on Criminal Justice,' 'Sunday Trading,' and 'Prosecution of Alleged War Criminals.' The latter articles were not attributed to any one individual but were published as the opinions of the Board for Social Responsibility, although the record contained a letter from the publishing company requesting the petitioner to prepare an article on the Church's position on Sunday Trading.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The database printout regarding the thesis did not indicate that it was published in a nationally circulated, peer-reviewed journal. The articles from the Board for Social Responsibility were not attributed to the petitioner individually. The NOID requested evidence that these articles were published in professional or major trade publications or other major media, but no additional evidence was received. Accordingly, the petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a list of members of the executive committee of the Society for the Study of Christian Ethics (SSCE) indicating he became a member of the committee in 1997, with the petitioner asserting SSCE is the main professional body in Great Britain for ethicists working in the Christian tradition and that the executive committee plans the annual conference and provides 'critical support' for the editor of Studies in Christian Ethics, a semi-annual publication of the society; (2) an assertion that as director of the Center for the Study of Theology at the University of Essex, he established a Masters program in theology at this traditionally secular school, supported by a pamphlet for the Masters program listing him as Director; (3) a University of Leeds Newsletter announcing the formation of Friends of the Department of Theology and Religious Studies at the University for which the petitioner agreed to serve as the first chairman; and (4) a letter indicating he was a 'senior member' of the board of the Society of Black Lawyers.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The NOID requested evidence of the distinguished reputation of the organizations and establishments for which the petitioner worked as well as documentation of the nature of the petitioner's exact role that would demonstrate he performed a leading or critical role. No supplemental evidence was submitted. Accordingly, the evidence was insufficient to meet this criterion, and the director correctly determined the petitioner did not meet it.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO affirmed the director's denial. An immigrant visa under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act will only be granted if the alien establishes extraordinary ability through extensive documentation of sustained national or international acclaim demonstrating that the alien has risen to the very top of the field. The petitioner bears a substantial burden of proof under Section 291 of the Act, and the petitioner in this case did not sustain that burden. The record did not establish that he is an alien of extraordinary ability, particularly given his failure to respond to the NOID and provide any of the requested supplemental evidence. The petitioner had apparently moved without informing CIS as required by statute, further compounding his evidentiary failures.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple procedural issues: (1) The petitioner apparently moved but failed to inform CIS of his new address as required by Section 265(a) of the Act and 8 C.F.R. § 265.1, resulting in the NOID, notice of certification, and two transfer notices being returned as 'Addressee Unknown. Attempted - Not Known.' (2) The prior AAO decision noted that signatures on photocopies of six letters submitted by the petitioner appeared to be digitally reproduced, and required the director to request submission of the original letters with the authors' original signatures pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(5); the director's NOID requested the original letters, but none were received. (3) The petitioner failed to respond to the NOID entirely, providing no supplemental evidence for any criterion. (4) The case has a complex procedural history involving a prior AAO remand and certification back to the AAO.
